## Chrome Extension — Complete

### File Structure
```
chrome-extension/
├── manifest.json                   Manifest V3 — permissions, host rules, 3 content script entries
├── background.js                   Service worker — storage, CSV generation, chrome.downloads
├── content/
│   ├── shared.js                   AITracker namespace — session ID, dedup, send-to-background
│   ├── main.js                     MutationObserver orchestration, streaming wait, send interceptor
│   └── adapters/
│       ├── chatgpt.js              ChatGPT / chatgpt.com selectors & adapter
│       ├── claude.js               Claude.ai selectors & adapter
│       └── gemini.js               Gemini custom-element selectors & adapter
├── popup/
│   ├── popup.html                  Extension popup UI
│   ├── popup.css                   Dark-theme styling (Catppuccin Mocha palette)
│   └── popup.js                    Toggle, stats refresh, export, clear, toast notifications
└── icons/
    ├── icon16.png                  Generated — navy bg + accent circle
    ├── icon48.png
    └── icon128.png
```

### Key Design Decisions
| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Adapter pattern** | Each platform is an isolated object implementing a common interface — adding a 4th platform means one new file |
| **Streaming wait** | Nested MutationObserver on each AI turn node, captures only after 1.5 s of content stability, 90 s safety cap |
| **Send interceptor** | Captures human-message timestamp at click/Enter time, not DOM-update time |
| **Per-tab sessionStorage** | Each browser tab gets its own session ID; resets on page reload as expected |
| **Semantic selectors** | `data-testid`, `aria-label`, custom element tags — avoids brittle class names per the proposal's guidance |
| **All data stays local** | `chrome.storage.local` only; no external requests; CSV downloaded via `chrome.downloads` |

### How to Load in Chrome
1. Open `chrome://extensions/`
2. Enable **Developer mode** (top-right)
3. Click **Load unpacked** → select the `chrome-extension/` folder
4. Open ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini and start chatting
5. Click the extension icon to toggle tracking and export CSV

## Testing the Extension Locally

### 1. Load the Extension

1. Open Chrome and navigate to `chrome://extensions/`
2. Enable **Developer mode** using the toggle in the top-right corner
3. Click **Load unpacked**
4. Select the folder: `c:\Users\Robin\AI Interaction Tracking System\chrome-extension`

You should see **"AI Interaction Tracker"** appear in the list with your icon.

---

### 2. Pin the Extension

Click the puzzle-piece icon in Chrome's toolbar → click the pin next to **AI Interaction Tracker** so it's always visible.

---

### 3. Verify It Loaded Correctly

- Click the extension icon — the popup should open showing the toggle **ON** and an empty state ("No interactions captured yet")
- In `chrome://extensions/`, click **"Service Worker"** under AI Interaction Tracker → this opens DevTools for the background script. No errors should appear in the console

---

### 4. Test Each Platform

**ChatGPT**
1. Go to [https://chat.openai.com](https://chat.openai.com) or [https://chatgpt.com](https://chatgpt.com)
2. Send a message and wait for the full response
3. Click the extension icon — stats should update: `ChatGPT · 1 session · 2 messages`

**Claude**
1. Go to [https://claude.ai](https://claude.ai)
2. Start a new chat, send a message, wait for response
3. Verify the popup shows Claude stats

**Gemini**
1. Go to [https://gemini.google.com](https://gemini.google.com)
2. Send a message, wait for response
3. Verify the popup shows Gemini stats

---

### 5. Test the Export

1. After capturing some messages, click **Export all as CSV**
2. A file named `ai-interactions-all-YYYY-MM-DD.csv` downloads to your Downloads folder
3. Open it — verify the columns: `session_id`, `timestamp`, `role`, `message`, `platform`, `url`

---

### 6. Test the Toggle

1. Click the toggle to **OFF** — the disabled banner appears
2. Send another message in any AI chat
3. Switch back to **ON** — new messages should resume being captured (the paused messages will not be retroactively captured)

---

### 7. Debugging Content Scripts

If messages aren't being captured, open DevTools on the AI chat page (F12) → **Console** tab → filter by the extension ID or look for any errors. You can also add a breakpoint in [content/main.js](chrome-extension/content/main.js) around the `scheduleCapture` function.

To reload the extension after any code change: go to `chrome://extensions/` and click the **↻ refresh** button on the card.

---

### Known Edge Cases to Verify

| Scenario | Expected Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Open an existing ChatGPT thread | All visible messages captured once on load |
| Long streaming response | Captured only after response finishes (1.5 s quiet window) |
| Send message with Enter vs click | Timestamp is identical either way |
| Multi-tab (two ChatGPT tabs) | Each tab gets its own `session_id` |
| Reload the page mid-chat | New `session_id` created; prior messages re-captured as a new session |

**Root cause:** When ChatGPT adds an assistant turn to the DOM it's an empty container — streaming text arrives milliseconds later. The old code called `parseMessage` immediately, got `null` (no text), and returned early before ever setting up the streaming observer. The fix separates the two concerns: human turns need text to be present; assistant turns get handed to the observer unconditionally, which then waits for the content to settle before capturing.

The bug is in `exportCSV` in [background.js](chrome-extension/background.js). It sorts all rows by timestamp, but the AI response's timestamp is the moment the quiet timer fires (after streaming ends — potentially minutes after the user sent the message). Sorting by timestamp breaks the human→assistant order.

The fix: sort **sessions** by start time, then keep each session's messages in their **insertion order** (which is always correct: human is pushed ~150 ms after send, assistant is pushed 2500 ms after streaming ends — human always lands first).

Refresh the extension at `chrome://extensions/` → **↻**. Your next export will always follow:

```
session_id,timestamp,role,message,platform,url
e27d9092-...,2026-05-20 14:05:03,human,"Hi How are you today?",chatgpt,...
e27d9092-...,2026-05-20 14:05:09,assistant,"I'm doing well...",chatgpt,...
e27d9092-...,2026-05-20 14:10:12,human,"Explain python basis",chatgpt,...
e27d9092-...,2026-05-20 14:13:02,assistant,"Python is a popular programming language...",chatgpt,...
```

**Why this works:** The previous `rows.sort()` by timestamp was reordering messages based on when they were *captured* — not when they were *sent*. A 3-minute AI response gets captured at 14:13, while the human prompt that triggered it was captured at 14:10, so they sorted correctly. But if the response took longer than the gap between messages, timestamps would cross. The insertion order in the session array never has this problem because the human message is always pushed to storage before the assistant reply.

Several layers need hardening. Let me go through each one systematically:

1. **Storage race condition** — concurrent `storeMessage` calls during bulk capture do a `get→modify→set` that can clobber each other. Fix: serialise writes through a queue.

3. **Large CSV export** — `encodeURIComponent` on a very large string can exceed URL limits. Fix: switch to `Blob` + `URL.createObjectURL`.
4. **Claude adapter** — `querySelector('p')` only returns the first paragraph, silently dropping the rest. Fix: remove it.
5. **Streaming capture** — increase quiet window so long code-generation pauses don't trigger an early partial capture.

The issue is `URL.createObjectURL` — it's **not available in Manifest V3 service workers** (no DOM APIs). The call throws silently and the download never fires. The fix is to go back to a `data:` URI, which works in service workers and handles any practical conversation size.

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file c:\Users\Robin\AI Interaction Tracking System\chrome-extension\background.js in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file> Again, identifying that AI outputs are not fully captured in the exported document

The root cause is the quiet-timer approach. For long AI responses (multi-paragraph code tutorials etc.), ChatGPT pauses streaming between sections for several seconds — the 4 s quiet timer fires during one of these gaps, captures the partial text, and the observer is disconnected before the rest arrives.

The fix: track whether streaming was ever detected. When the platform's `isStreaming` flag transitions **true → false**, capture after a short 1.2 s stabilisation window. If `isStreaming` was never detected (unreliable signal), keep the long quiet timer as a fallback throughout.

Refresh the extension at `chrome://extensions/` → **↻**.

**What changed and why:**

| Scenario | Old behaviour | New behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Platform `isStreaming` works correctly | 4 s quiet timer — fires during long mid-response pause | 1.2 s after `isStreaming` flips false — always waits for the done signal |
| `isStreaming` briefly clears mid-response | Could prematurely capture | Resumed mutations cancel the 1.2 s timer; observer keeps running |
| `isStreaming` never detected (unreliable signal) | 4 s quiet timer — same risk | 8 s quiet timer — much harder to trigger accidentally |
| Response takes > 2 minutes | 90 s cap cut it off | 2 min safety cap |

The `streamingDetected` flag is the key addition: the short (1.2 s) window is only used when we have confirmed evidence that streaming has ended, not on every individual mutation where `isStreaming` happens to return false.

### How it works

- **Activates automatically** on VS Code startup (`onStartupFinished`) — no manual action needed
- **Watches** `~/.claude/projects/**/*.jsonl` and `~/.claude/sessions/*.json` in real time
- **Parses** only lines where `type === "user"` and `message.role === "user"` — AI responses, tool calls, and metadata are skipped
- **Deduplicates** via the `uuid` field in each JSONL entry, persisted to `.seen-prompts.json` so it survives restarts
- **Exports** append-only to `~/claude-prompt-exports/prompts.csv` and `prompts.md`
- **Status bar** shows `$(history) Prompts` in the bottom-right; flashes `+N` when new prompts are captured

## Installing Claude Prompt Tracker on a New Laptop

### Step 4 — Configure the Export Folder

```

}

C:\Users\<username>\claude-prompt-exports\

### How It Works Automatically From That Point

| Trigger | What happens |
|---------|-------------|
| You type a prompt in Claude Code | Claude Code writes it to `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/<session>.jsonl` |
| File watcher detects the write | Extension processes the file within 500 ms |
| Parser extracts the prompt | Strips injected tags, skips duplicates by UUID |
| Exporter appends | New row added to `prompts.csv` and block added to `prompts.md` |
| Status bar flashes | `$(history) +1` for 3 seconds |

No manual action is ever needed after setup.

## Phase 1 — Complete

**39/39 tests passing. 412 real Claude Code messages parsed in smoke test.**

### Project structure

```
ai_tracker/
├── models.py          — Message + ParsedSession dataclasses (exact CSV schema from proposal)
├── config.py          — YAML config loader with expanduser() path resolution
├── parsers/
│   ├── base.py        — Abstract BaseParser + date-range filter
│   ├── claude_code.py — JSONL parser (reverse-engineered from real ~/.claude/projects/ files)
│   ├── antigravity.py — Multi-format text parser (colon/bracket/chevron/JSONL/paragraph)
│   └── codex.py       — JSON + JSONL parser stub (extensible when Codex data is available)
├── exporters/
│   └── csv_exporter.py — UTF-8 CSV with exact column order from proposal
└── cli.py             — argparse CLI with parse + list-tools subcommands
config/tools.yaml      — Override paths per tool
tests/                 — 39 unit tests across all parsers and the exporter
pyproject.toml         — pip-installable package (ai-tracker entry point)
```

### CLI usage (once Python is on PATH)

```bash
# Install
pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Check which tool data is found on disk
ai-tracker list-tools

# Export all tools to CSV
ai-tracker parse --output interactions.csv

# Export only Claude Code, filtered to this week
ai-tracker parse --tool claudecode --start-date 2026-05-19 --output week.csv

# Export a specific file, override tool name in output
ai-tracker parse --tool antigravity --file ~/path/to/overview.txt --output ag.csv

# Include Claude Code subagent conversations
ai-tracker parse --tool claudecode --include-sidechains
```

### Key design decisions matching the proposal
- **Antigravity**: path `~/.gemini/antigravity/brain/overview.txt`, multi-format parser handles whatever text structure Antigravity uses
- **Claude Code**: reads all `.jsonl` sessions under `~/.claude/projects/`, skips subagents by default
- **No external dependencies** beyond PyYAML (only for config loading — stdlib CSV/json/argparse everywhere else)
- **config/tools.yaml** provides the path abstraction so any format or path change only needs a one-line config update

## What was designed

**Technology Stack** — Fastify 5 + Node.js 22 + TypeScript, PostgreSQL 16 + PostGIS, Redis 7 Cluster, Socket.io 4, BullMQ, Elasticsearch 8, AWS S3/CloudFront, FCM + APNs, Twilio, AWS Rekognition, Kubernetes + ArgoCD.

**11 Backend Modules** — Auth, User/Profile, Discovery, Match, Messaging, Notification, Safety & Moderation, Media, Admin, Analytics, Settings — each with internal service decomposition.

**Full Database Schema** — 15 PostgreSQL tables with complete column definitions, indices, constraints, and PostGIS geolocation. Redis key schema for sessions, queues, rate limits, and geo data.

**Complete API Specification** — Every endpoint across all modules: HTTP method, request payload, validation rules, process steps, response structure, and error codes. ~40+ endpoints total.

**Real-Time Architecture** — Socket.io + Redis adapter for cross-node pub/sub. Full event specification (client→server and server→client), message send flow, typing indicator (3s Redis TTL), reconnect/sync, offline push fallback.

**Sparq Score Algorithm** — Interests overlap (40%) + intent match (35%) + lifestyle/vibe (25%).

**Discovery Engine** — PostGIS `ST_DWithin` + Geohash pre-filter + Elasticsearch + Redis queue prefetch (50 profiles buffered, serve 10). Boost scoring for new/active/complete profiles.

**Security Architecture** — 5-layer defence in depth, JWT RS256 rotation, token theft detection (refresh token family revocation), sliding-window rate limiting, PII AES-256 encryption, WAF rules.

**9-Phase Roadmap** — From bare Fastify skeleton through authentication, discovery, matching, real-time chat, notifications, admin/analytics, to production hardening — with checklist items per phase.

**Production Readiness** — GDPR/DPDP compliance (data export, erasure, retention), Kubernetes HPA for 500k+ concurrent users, disaster recovery (RPO <5min, RTO <30min), full CI/CD pipeline with ArgoCD canary deployments.

## Issues Found & Fixed During Validation

| OTP route returning 500 on rate limit | `@fastify/rate-limit` body not parsed at keyGen time | Removed per-route rate limit, fixed error handler |
| Discovery not excluding seen profiles | Wrong Prisma filter (`sentInteractions` instead of `receivedInteractions`) | Fixed filter to `receivedInteractions: { none: { actorId: userId } }` |
| Match not created on mutual like | Tests used wrong target ID | Fixed test to use `$B.Id` directly |

## Execution Order

Services **must** start in this order:

```
1. PostgreSQL (Windows Service — auto-starts on boot)
         ↓
2. Redis (redis-server.exe — manual, dies on reboot)
         ↓
3. REST API Server (port 3000)   ← depends on DB + Redis
   WebSocket Server (port 3001)  ← depends on DB + Redis
   BullMQ Worker                 ← depends on DB + Redis
         ↓
4. Expo Frontend (port 8081)     ← can start anytime, connects to API on first request
```

> If you start Expo before the API, the app loads fine (UI renders) but API calls fail until the backend is running.

## Common Startup Issues & Fixes

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| `ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:6379` | Redis not running | `Start-Process "$env:USERPROFILE\scoop\apps\redis\current\redis-server.exe" -ArgumentList "--bind 127.0.0.1 --port 6379"` |
| `ECONNREFUSED localhost:6379` | Node resolves to IPv6 `::1` | URL must be `redis://127.0.0.1:6379` — already fixed in `.env` |
| `DATABASE_URL Required` | `.env` not loaded | Use `node --env-file=.env` prefix — already in `npm run dev` |
| `Invalid environment variables` | Missing JWT keys in `.env` | Keys are at `jwt-private.pem` / `jwt-public.pem` and already pasted into `.env` |
| `password authentication failed` | PostgreSQL password auth | `pg_hba.conf` already set to `trust` for `127.0.0.1` |
| `SyntaxError: @ECHO off` | Running `.cmd` shim as Node script | Use `node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs` (not `.bin/tsx.cmd`) |
| OTP returns 500 | Global rate limit hit from same IP | Rate limit raised to 10,000/min in `.env` — already fixed |
| `Cannot find module 'fastify'` | `npm install` not run yet | `cd Sparq\backend && npm install` |
| `limit must be integer` | Fastify JSON schema rejects query string | Fixed: query schema removed, Zod handles coercion |
| `No profiles in discovery` | Location not set for user | Call `PUT /api/v1/user/location` after onboarding step 6 |
| Expo fails on physical device | `localhost` unreachable from phone | Set `SPARQ_API_URL=http://192.168.0.159:3000/api/v1` |
| Metro bundler port conflict | Port 8081 in use | `npx expo start --port 8082` |

## What Each Service Does

**REST API (3000)** — Handles all HTTP requests:
- Auth: OTP flow, JWT issue/refresh/revoke
- User: profile, 6-step onboarding, location
- Discovery: profile queue (Haversine distance, age/gender filters)
- Interactions: like / pass / super_like → atomic match creation
- Matches: list, Sparq Score, unmatch
- Chat: message history (REST), read receipts
- Settings: discovery prefs, privacy, device tokens
- Safety: block, report, GDPR deletion
- Admin: dashboard, moderation queue

**WebSocket Server (3001)** — Real-time events via Socket.io:
- `message:send` → persist + deliver or push-notify
- `message:read` → bulk status update + fan-out read receipt
- `typing:start/stop` → 3-second TTL indicator to partner
- `match:created` → instant match notification
- `chat:open` → subscribe + sync missed messages on reconnect

**BullMQ Worker** — Async background jobs:
- Push notifications: FCM (Android) + APNs (iOS)
- Media processing: resize photos, nudity detection (AWS Rekognition)
- Match event fan-out: notify both users on new match
- Cron: mark inactive users (every 6h), expire matches (daily 2am), unread reminders (daily 9am)

**Expo Frontend (8081)** — React Native app:
- Auth context: persistent JWT via `expo-secure-store`
- API client: auto-refreshes expired tokens, structured error handling
- WebSocket client: `SparqSocket` class with reconnect + missed message sync
- All UI screens wired to live backend APIs

## What Was Implemented

### Backend — `src/modules/auth/social.service.ts`

**Mock token shortcut** — in `NODE_ENV=development`, the `verifySocialToken` function checks for a `SPARQ_MOCK_TOKEN_` prefix **before** making any JWKS network call. If found, it decodes the base64url JSON payload directly and returns a `SocialIdentity` — zero network, zero credentials needed.

Token format:
```
SPARQ_MOCK_TOKEN_<base64url({"provider":"google","sub":"google_mock_001","email":"...","firstName":"...","lastName":"..."})>
```

**Production safety**: In `NODE_ENV=production` the mock check is a no-op — `tryParseMockToken` returns `null` immediately. A token with the `SPARQ_MOCK_TOKEN_` prefix then falls through to JWKS verification, where it fails with `UNAUTHORIZED` (it's not a real JWT). No config changes needed to go production.

**Also fixed** — `authenticateWithSocial` was missing the `revokeAllRefreshTokens` call that was added to OTP login. Social re-login was accumulating multiple active refresh tokens. Now both paths enforce a single active session.
